content:
# Optical anisotropy of uniaxially drawn and silver-dispersed polyimide films

Takashi Sawada, $^{a)}$  Shinji Ando, $^{b)}$  and Shigekuni Sasaki

NTT Science and Core Technology Laboratory Group, 3-9-11 Midori-cho, Musashino-shi, Tokyo,

180-8585, Japan

(Received 17 August 1998; accepted for publication 10 December 1998)

Distinct anisotropy in optical transmittance in the visible and near-infrared region for uniaxially drawn and silver-dispersed polyimide films was observed. The films were prepared in a one-step operation that involves thermal curing and simultaneous uniaxial drawing of poly(amic acid) (PAA) films, which were made by dissolving silver nitrate in the PAA solution at a 1:4 mol ratio. The polyimide molecular chains with a rod-like structure were oriented along the drawing direction during curing, and this orientation accompanied the generation of silver nanoparticles with elongated shapes. An anisotropy in the optical transmittance of 5:1 was obtained for a  $22 - \mu \mathrm{m}$ -thick film at  $850\mathrm{nm}$  with transmittance of  $68\%$  perpendicular to the drawing direction. The optical and mechanical properties of this film were retained after annealing at  $300^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  for  $1\mathrm{h}$ . © 1999

American Institute of Physics. [S0003-6951(99)02107-5]

It has been reported that the polyimide films derived from pyromellitic dianhydride and  $2,2^{\prime}$ -bis(trifluoromethyl)- $4,4^{\prime}$ -diaminobiphenyl (PMDA/TFDB, Fig. 1), which has a rod-like structure, shows a large anisotropy in refractive indices in the film plane (in-plane birefringence:  $\Delta n$ ) by uniaxial drawing during thermal curing. $^{1}$  Comparing with conventional unfluorinated polyimides, this material shows higher transparency in the visible and near-infrared (IR) regions as well as lower dielectric constant, lower refractive indices, and lower water absorption. $^{2}$  The largest  $\Delta n$  of 0.21 was observed at  $1543~\mathrm{nm}$ , $^{3}$  and a thin optical half-waveplate at  $1555~\mathrm{nm}$ , only  $14.5~\mu \mathrm{m}$  thick, which is 6.3 times thinner than a zeroth-order quartz waveplate, was prepared using this polyimide. $^{4,5}$  The  $\Delta n$  of the waveplate was retained after annealing at  $350^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  for  $1\mathrm{h}$ , indicating that the chemical structure and the molecular ordering of the polyimide were maintained up to  $350^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

Polyimide films containing metal/metal oxide particles generated in situ have been studied extensively by Taylor and co-workers. They have also incorporated silver salts and organosilver complexes in aromatic polyimides and obtained highly reflective silver-containing polyimide films. We have recently prepared five kinds of metal [aluminum, copper, palladium, silver(Ag), and gold(Au)]-containing fluorinated polyimide films in order to develop thermally stable polymeric materials that exhibit optical functions and high transparency in the near-infrared region. Although all the films show much less transparency in the visible region than the host polyimide, their transmittance in the near-infrared region (at  $1550\mathrm{nm}$ ) is higher than  $80\%$  except for the Au-containing film.

Stookey and Araujo<sup>11</sup> first observed a large anisotropy in optical transmittance (polarizing property) in a stretched inorganic glass containing silver particles. They confirmed that the anisotropy originates from the differences in the selective

absorption wavelengths parallel and perpendicular to the longer axis of elongated metal particles and, thus, the wavelengths with high extinction ratio (that is the difference in transmittance between two polarizing directions) can be shifted by changing the size and the anisotropy of the silver particles. Using this principle, Gritz<sup>12</sup> fabricated high-efficiency glass polarizers with transmittance higher than  $90\%$  and contrast larger than 500:1. Baba and co-workers<sup>13-15</sup> investigated the optical anisotropy caused by the elongated silver islands embedded in glasses. In our study, in a one-step operation, we generated silver nanoparticles having elongated shapes in a thermally stable polymeric material. The preparation method is schematically shown in Fig. 2. As shown by Taylor and co-workers<sup>7-9</sup> and these authors,<sup>10</sup> silver salts and organosilver complexes are reduced to the metal state and precipitated as silver nanoparticles in polyimide films by thermal curing. We confirmed by transmission electron micrographs that the silver particles precipitated in a rod-like fluorinated polyimide had spherical shapes.<sup>10</sup> This indicates that the local molecular structure of polyimide does not affect the shape of the precipitated particles. However, when the polyimide molecular chains are highly oriented by uniaxial drawing during curing, the shapes of the silver particles are expected to be elongated along the drawing direction.

The fluorinated polyimide, PMDA/TFDB, was used as a matrix polymer in this study because of its properties described above. The preparation of  $N,N$ -dimethylacetamide solution of poly(amic acid) (PAA), the precursor of polyimide, has been described elsewhere. Silver nitrate  $(\mathrm{AgNO}_3)$

![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片)  
PMDA / TFDB  
FIG. 1. Structural formula for rod-like fluorinated polyimide.

![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片)  
FIG. 2. Method for preparing optically anisotropic polyimide films.

was used as a source material of silver particles because it has good solubility in PAA solution. After drying at  $110^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  for  $1\mathrm{h}$ ,  $\mathrm{AgNO}_3$  was dissolved in the PAA solution under nitrogen.  $\mathrm{AgNO}_3$  was added into the PAAs in a 1:4 mol ratio. The solutions were stirred for  $12\mathrm{h}$  at room temperature and spin coated onto a 4 in. silicon wafer, followed by drying in nitrogen at  $70^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  for  $1\mathrm{h}$ .

Figure 3 shows the absorption spectra of the silver-doped PAA and polyimide films cured on silica substrates at various temperatures for  $1\mathrm{h}$ . No optical anisotropy was observed in the film plane for all the films. An absorption peak is clearly observed at  $430~\mathrm{nm}$  for the film cured at  $200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . This peak wavelength coincides with that of the surface plasmon resonance of Ag colloids reported in the literature.[16,17] This indicates that  $\mathrm{AgNO_3}$  incorporated into polyimide was reduced to Ag by the thermal treatment at  $200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  and, consequently, silver nanoparticles were generated. As the curing temperature increases, the absorption band at  $430~\mathrm{nm}$  grows and looks slightly displaced to longer wavelengths, suggesting that the silver particles increased in size. The different shape of the spectrum that was observed for the film cured at  $350^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  can be understood from its appearance; a reflective surface with silvery color was formed on the air-side surface.

![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片)  
FIG. 3. Changes in absorption spectra of fluorinated poly(amic acid) films added  $\mathrm{AgNO_3}$  for final curing temperatures of  $70 - 350^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . At around  $200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the poly(amic acid) is thermally converted to polyimide, and Ag nanoparticles begin to coagulate.

![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片)  
FIG. 4. Optical transmittance spectra of  $22 - \mu \mathrm{m}$  -thick uniaxially drawn Ag-nanoparticle-dispersed and non-dispersed polyimide films for normally incident light polarized perpendicular  $(T_{\perp})$  and parallel  $(T_{\parallel})$  to the drawing direction. The heating rate was  $10^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}$ , and the load ranged from 5 to  $18\mathrm{g}$ .

This indicates that Ag particles were concentrated near the air-side surface. Since the formation of a reflective surface is undesirable for the use of films as an optically anisotropic film such as a polarizer, thermal curing below  $350^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  is preferable for that purpose.

For preparing uniaxially drawn silver-dispersed polyimide films, PAA films peeled from the substrate were cut into rectangles of  $15 \times 5 \mathrm{~mm}$  and loaded in a thermal mechanical analyzer (TMA: Sinku-Riko TM-7000). A constant load was applied along the longer axis of the film by the TMA during thermal curing. The film was thus heated to  $320^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$  at a heating rate of 5 and  $10^{\circ} \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{min}$ , kept at this temperature for  $1 \mathrm{~h}$  and then cooled to room temperature. The optical anisotropy of the films was measured by rotating a Glan-Thompson prism (polarizer) inserted into the light path of a spectrophotometer (Hitachi U-3500, wavelengths:  $600 - 1300 \mathrm{~nm}$ ). The average thicknesses of the polyimide films were  $22 \mu \mathrm{m}$ .

Figure 4 shows the transmittance spectra of the silver-dispersed polyimide films uniaxially drawn during curing at the heating rate of  $10^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}$  with varying the load of the TMA from 5 to  $18\mathrm{g}$ . All films prepared at  $5^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}$  were cut around  $300^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . The spectrum of the polyimide film prepared from undoped PAA is also shown for comparison. The nonsilver-dispersed film showed much higher transmittance in a wide range but no optical anisotropy. All the uniaxially drawn and silver-dispersed films, on the other hand, exhibited distinct anisotropy in optical transmittance in the visible and near-IR region. The transmittance parallel to the drawing direction is considerably lower than that perpendicular to it. This phenomenon can be explained by the same mechanism as that for stretched glasses containing silver particles. In addition, the extinction ratios increased as the load increased. An extinction ratio of  $7\mathrm{dB}$  (1:5 in transmittance ratio) was obtained at  $850~\mathrm{nm}$  for a film cured with a load of  $18\mathrm{g}$ .

Temperature dependence of elongation behavior of the films is shown in Fig. 5. Compared with the undoped PAA, the doped PAA films showed larger shrinkage between 100 and  $300^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  and sudden elongation above  $300^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . In addition, the elongation increased as the load increased. The magnitude of the sudden elongation should be important for induc

![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片)  
FIG. 5. Elongation behaviors of  $\mathrm{AgNO_3}$ -doped and undoped poly(amic acid) films for various loads. The final curing temperature was  $320^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

ing a high degree of molecular orientation of polyimides, which is the main cause that generates silver particles with elongated shapes.

A high-resolution reflection image of an uniaxially drawn film (18 g in Fig. 5) observed by scanning electron micrograph (JEOL JSM-890) is shown in Fig. 6. Some of the particles have elongated shapes and their longer axes are oriented to the drawing direction. The sizes of these particles were estimated to be several tens of nm along the longer axes and several nm along the shorter axes. The difference in transmittance between the two polarization directions and the wavelength dispersion of optical anisotropy should be determined by size, aspect ratio, degree of orientation of the longer axis, and anisotropic distribution of the silver particles precipitated in a film. Hence, the wavelength dependence of the optical transmittance and anisotropy can be controlled by varying the curing conditions, the drawing conditions, and the kind of dopant.

In this study, the highest curing temperature of silver-dispersed polyimide films was  $310 - 340^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  lower than that used for inorganic glass matrices, $^{14}$  and the highest temperature was kept for  $1\mathrm{h}$  after the elongation of the films. It is surprising that the elongated shapes of the silver particles in

![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片)  
FIG. 6. High-resolution reflection image of Ag-nanoparticle-dispersed film observed using scanning electron micrography.

the polyimide films do not relax to spheres at the elongation temperature of  $320^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . This is due the low elongation temperature of  $\mathrm{Ag}$  particles and the high molecular order of the surrounding polyimide chains. In the conventional processes, metal nanoparticles are formed or embedded in a matrix glass and then elongated by uniaxial drawing with the glass plate. However, in the present method, the precipitation and elongation of silver nanoparticles take place in one step. The fact that an absorption peak of silver nanoparticles was observed even for an isotropic film cured at  $200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and that it grew considerably at higher temperatures, suggests the following mechanism: (a) silver nanoparticles, which are likely to have spherical shapes, begin to precipitate from polyimide around  $200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ; (b) the precipitation become vigorous, and the particles begin to coagulate or sinter between 250 and  $300^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ; and (c) spherical silver particles are elongated along the drawing direction between 300 and  $320^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  while the polyimide film significantly elongates. Uniaxially oriented polyimide molecules provide an anisotropic environment for the growth of silver nanoparticles. The melting temperature of bulk silver is higher than  $900^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ; however, it has been reported that sintering of silver nanoparticles begins at  $150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

The silver-dispersed elongated polyimide films showed good flexibility and tractability although they were cleavable along the drawing direction. The thickness of the films ( $\sim 22 \mu \mathrm{m}$ ) is sufficiently thin for decreasing the excess loss caused when films are inserted into a groove.[4,5] Furthermore, no change was observed in the transmittance spectrum, the optical anisotropy, and the flexibility after annealing at  $300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$  for  $1 \mathrm{~h}$ . The uniaxially drawn and silver-dispersed polyimide films affords thin, flexible, and thermally stable films that exhibit anisotropy in optical transmittance in the visible and near-infrared region.

$^{1}$ S. Ando, T. Sawada, and Y. Inoue, in *Polymeric Materials for Microelectronic Applications*, edited by H. Ito, S. Tagawa, and K. Horie, ACS Symp. Ser. 579 (American Chemical Society, Washington, DC, 1994), p. 283.  
$^{2}$ T. Matsuura, Y. Hasuda, S. Nishi, and Y. Yamada, Macromolecules 24, 5001 (1991).  
$^{3}$ S. Ando, T. Sawada, and S. Sasaki, Polym. Adv. Technol. 9 (in press).  
4S. Ando, T. Sawada, and Y. Inoue, Electron. Lett. 29, 2143 (1993).  
$^{5}$ Y. Inoue, H. Takahashi, S. Ando, T. Sawada, A. Himeno, and M. Kawachi, J. Lightwave Technol. 15, 1947 (1997).  
For example, M. W. Ellison and L. T. Taylor, Chem. Mater. 6, 990 (1994), and references therein.  
7 A. K. St. Clair and L. T. Taylor, J. Appl. Polym. Sci. 28, 2393 (1983).  
$^{8}$ J. D. Rancourt, G. M. Porta, and L. T. Taylor, in Proc. 19th Intern. SAMPE Tech. Conf., 13 (1987).  
$^{9}$ A. F. Rubira, J. D. Rancourt, M. L. Caplan, A. K. St. Clair, and L. T. Taylor, Chem. Mater. 6, 2351 (1994).  
10T. Sawada and S. Ando, Chem. Mater. 6, 2351 (1994).  
11 S. D. Stoovey and R. J. Araujo, Appl. Opt. 7, 777 (1968).  
$^{12}$ D. N. Gritz, Proc. SPIE 750, (1987).  
13K. Baba and M. Miyagi, Opt. Lett. 16, 964 (1991).  
$^{14}$ K. Baba, J. Katsu, and M. Miyagi, Opt. Lett. 17, 622 (1992).  
15M. Takakuwa, K. Baba, and M. Miyagi, Opt. Lett. 21, 1195 (1996).  
16D. Richard, P. Roussignol, and C. Flytzanis, Opt. Lett. 10, 511 (1985).  
$^{17}$ S. Ogawa, Y. Hayashi, N. Kobayashi, T. Tokizaki, and A. Nakamura, Jpn. J. Appl. Phys., Part 2 33, L331 (1994).  
$^{18}$ R. Radebaugh, J. D. Siegwarth, and J. C. Holste, in Proc. Fifth Intern. Cryogenic Eng. Conf., H6-1 (1974).

Article

# Carborane-Containing Aromatic Polyimide Films with Ultrahigh Thermo-Oxidative Stability

Fulin Liu  $^{1,2}$ , Guangqiang Fang  $^{3}$ , Haixia Yang  $^{1,*}$ , Shiyong Yang  $^{1,*}$ , Xuezhong Zhang  $^{1}$  and Zhijie Zhang  $^{1}$

$^{1}$  Laboratory of Advanced Polymer Materials, Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100190, China; liufulin@iccas.ac.cn (F.L.); zhangxuezhong@iccas.ac.cn (X.Z.); zhangzj@iccas.ac.cn (Z.Z.)  
2 School of Chemical Sciences, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, China  
3 Shanghai Institute of Aerospace Systems Engineering, Shanghai 201108, China; house19@126.com  
* Correspondence: yanghx@iccas.ac.cn (H.Y.); shiyang@iccas.ac.cn (S.Y.)

Received: 28 October 2019; Accepted: 12 November 2019; Published: 22 November 2019

![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片)

check for updates

Abstract: Carborane-containing aromatic polyimide (CPI) films with ultrahigh thermo-oxidative stability at  $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  have been prepared by casting poly(amic acid) (PAA) resin solution on a glass surface, followed by thermal imidization at elevated temperatures. The PAA solution was prepared by copolymerization of an aromatic dianhydride and an aromatic diamine mixture, including carborane-containing aromatic diamine in an aprotic solvent. The CPI films showed excellent thermo-oxidative stability at  $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  due to the multilayered protection layers formed on the film surface by thermal conversion of the carborane group into boron oxides. The boron oxide layer enhanced the degradation activation energy and suppressed the direct contact of inner polymer materials with oxygen molecules in a high-temperature environment, acting as a "self-healing" skin layer on the polyimide materials. The CPI-50 film was still flexible and maintained  $50\%$  retention of mechanical strength even after thermo-oxidative aging at  $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}/5\mathrm{min}$ . The mechanism of thermo-oxidative degradation was proposed.

Keywords: polyimide; carborane; thermo-oxidative stability; self-healing layer

file_name: Carborane-Containing Aromatic Polyimide Films with Ultrahigh Thermo-Oxidative Stability.md
content:
# 1. Introduction

Aromatic polyimide films have been extensively utilized in a variety of applications from microelectronic and optoelectronic devices to aerospace industries, owing to its excellent thermal stability, dielectric and electrical insulating performance, mechanical properties, and chemical and radiation resistance [1-7]. With the rapid development of high-tech fields, polyimide films have also been attempted to be used as gas separation membranes [8-11], battery separators [12-15], thermally conductive films [16-18], transparent electrodes [19-22], etc. Although polyimide is one of the most thermal stable polymers, it starts rapid thermal or thermo-oxidative decomposition at the beginning from  $500 - 550^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  and loses most of its original weight at about  $600 - 650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , which restricts its application in extreme environments, such as engine culverts in airplanes, nuclear power stations, and submarines. In those circumstances, high temperatures and repeated thermal cycles require thermal and thermos-oxidative stability of materials at elevated temperatures. In order to improve the thermal or thermo-oxidative stability, polyimide films are usually coated with metal or inorganic oxide protection layer or blended with inorganic fillers [23-27]. However, the mismatching of the coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE) between inorganic coatings and polymer matrices might result in serious troubles such as delamination, curl, or crack after harsh heat shocking [28]. Although the addition of inorganic fillers into polymer matrices to form hybrid structures is an effective pathway in enhancing

thermo-oxidative stability of polymer materials, the limited filler loadings and the poor miscibility of inorganic fillers and organic matrices are the big issues [26,27,29,30]. Hence, developing intrinsic thermo-oxidative stable polymers is still the desirable pathway.

Incorporating the carborane  $(\mathrm{C}_2\mathrm{B}_{10}\mathrm{H}_{12})$  cage structure into the polymer backbone has been found to provide a promising pathway to improve the thermo-oxidative stability of polymer materials due to the reaction of carborane with oxygen atoms to yield a boron oxide layer [31]. The icosahedral carborane structure has two carbon atoms in a symmetrical polyhedral cage, exhibiting three-dimensional aromaticity [32-34]. Y.P. Bai et al. synthesized wholly carborane-containing polyesters exhibiting a char yield of  $64\%$  in air and  $47\%$  in N2 at  $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , respectively, much superior to the carborane-free polyesters [35]. Zh. J. Zhang et al. synthesized a series of exactly alternating carborane-siloxane polymers showing excellent thermal stability with char yields of  $\geq 83\%$  both in air at  $800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  and in nitrogen at  $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , respectively [36]. X. D. Jia et al. introduced a meta-carborane structure into the polybenzoxazine main-chain via click reaction showing a  $5\%$  weight loss at temperatures above  $400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  and high char yield of  $76\%$  in air at  $800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  [37].

In this paper, a series of carborane-containing aromatic polyimide (CPI) films with carborane cage structures in the polymer backbone have been prepared by copolymerization of aromatic dianhydride and a mixture of aromatic diamines, including 1,7-bis(aminophenyl)-meta-carborane. Experimental results indicated that the carborane containing polyimide films demonstrated great thermo-oxidative stability at temperatures of as high as  $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . The effect of polyimide structures on the thermal properties have been systematically investigated, and a mechanism of thermo-oxidative degradation was proposed. Combined with the neutron-trapping ability of the boron atoms, the thermal-oxidative stable CPI we reported were potential candidates in nuclear power applications.

file_name: Carborane-Containing Aromatic Polyimide Films with Ultrahigh Thermo-Oxidative Stability.md
content:
# 2. Materials and Methods

# 2.1. Materials

$3,3^{\prime},4,4^{\prime}$ -biphenyltetracarboxylic dianhydride (s-BPDA),  $4,4^{\prime}$ -diaminodiphenyl ether  $(4,4^{\prime}$ -ODA), and 1,4-benzenediamine (PDA) were obtained from Changzhou Sunlight Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd. (Changzhou, China), and dried in a vacuum at  $160^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ,  $80^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ,  $80^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  for  $12\mathrm{h}$  before use, respectively. The 1,7-bis(aminophenyl)-meta-carborane (BACB) was synthesized in our lab, and the details are shown in Scheme S1 in Supporting Information. N-methyl-2-pyrrolidinone (NMP) was purchased from J&K Scientific Co. Ltd. (Beijing, China), then distilled under reduced pressure with  $\mathrm{CaH_2}$  and stored with  $4\AA$  molecular sieves before use. Other solvents and reagents were directly used without further purification.

# 2.2. Synthesis of the Carborane-Containing Polyimide Films

According to previous research,  $\mathrm{n(PDA) / n(4,4^{\prime} - ODA)} = 7 / 3$  was the optimal molar ratio for thermal stability and mechanical property of the PI film to be in equilibrium [38]. Thus, the molar ratio of PDA and  $4,4^{\prime}$ -ODA was kept at 7/3 for all PI systems in this research. A typical polymerization process of CPI-20 was prepared in the following procedure: in a dried three-necked flask, equipped with an electric agitation, nitrogen gas inlet and outlet, PDA (1.8168 g, 16.8 mmol),  $4,4^{\prime}$ -ODA (1.4417 g, 7.2 mmol), BACB (1.9586 g, 6 mmol), and part of anhydrous NMP (32 mL) were added. The mixture was stirred at room temperature until the diamine was completely dissolved, then an equimolar amount of dianhydride s-BPDA (8.8266 g, 30 mmol) and residual NMP (10 mL) were added into the solution to adjust the solid content to approximately 25 wt.% The mixture was continuously stirred at room temperature in  $\mathbf{N}_2$  atmosphere for a further  $48\mathrm{h}$  to get a homogeneous poly(amic acid) (PAA) solution. Then the viscous PAA solution was filtrated by G1 sand cored funnel and degassed under vacuum for  $2\mathrm{h}$  in order to prepare a defect-free film. Subsequently, the PAA solution was coated onto a pre-cleaned glass plate and thermally imidized according to the following schedule:  $120^{\circ}\mathrm{C}/2\mathrm{h}$ ,  $200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}/1\mathrm{h}$ ,  $300^{\circ}\mathrm{C}/1\mathrm{h}$ ,  $400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}/0.5\mathrm{h}$  with a heating rate of  $2^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\min^{-1}$  to prepare the self-supporting

films. As a consequence, reddish-brown CPI films with a thickness of about  $50~\mu \mathrm{m}$  were obtained. This series of CPI films were named as CPI- $x$ , in which  $x$  represents the molar ratio of BACB. As a comparison, CPI-0 was based on s-BPDA, 4,4'-ODA, and PDA without any BACB, and its preparation and imidization processes were the same as above.

# 2.3. Characterization and Measurements

FT-IR spectra were measured in the range of  $400 - 4000\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$  on a Tensor-27 Fourier transform spectrophotometer (Bruker, Germany).  ${}^{1}\mathrm{H}$  NMR spectra of PAA resins were acquired on a  $400\mathrm{MHz}$  Avance 400 spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) in deuterated dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO- $d_6$ ). The elemental analysis of PAA resins was carried out with a Varlo ELIII elemental (Elementar, Langenselbold, Germany). The mechanical properties were conducted on an Instron 5567 universal testing machine (Instron, Norwood, MA, USA) with a specimen size of  $80\times 10\times 0.05\mathrm{mm}^3$  in agreement with GB1447-2005 at a drawing rate of  $2.0\mathrm{mm}\mathrm{min}^{-1}$ . Dynamic mechanical analyses were performed on a DMA Q800 thermal analysis system (TA, USA) with a heating rate of  $5^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\mathrm{min}^{-1}$  under nitrogen atmosphere at a flow rate of  $40\mathrm{cm}^3\mathrm{min}^{-1}$ . Thermogravimetric analyses were recorded on a TGA Q50 thermal analysis system (TA, USA) with a heating rate of  $20^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\mathrm{min}^{-1}$  under both nitrogen and air atmosphere at a flow rate of  $40\mathrm{cm}^3\mathrm{min}^{-1}$ . Thermo-oxidative aging was also carried out on a TGA Q50 thermogravimetric analyzer with a heating rate of  $50^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\mathrm{min}^{-1}$  ramping from 50 to  $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  and isothermal aging in air at  $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  for  $150\mathrm{min}$ . Surface chemistry of CPI samples and PAA resins were investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, VG ESCALab 250Xi, USA) using 200 W monochromatic Al Kα radiation. Surfaces morphologies of the PI films were imaged by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi S-4800, Tokyo, Japan) at  $15\mathrm{kV}$  voltage.

file_name: Carborane-Containing Aromatic Polyimide Films with Ultrahigh Thermo-Oxidative Stability.md
content:
# 3. Results and Discussions # 3.1. Preparation and Characterization of CPI Films The polyimide precursor resin—PAA resin solution was synthesized by polycondensation of equimolar aromatic dianhydride (s-BPDA) with a mixture of three different aromatic diamines, including PDA, $4,4^{\prime}$ -ODA, and 1,7-bis(aminophenyl)-meta-carborane (BACB) in NMP (Figure 1a). The PAA solution was first coated on glass surface, followed by prebaking at $120^{\circ}\mathrm{C} / 2\mathrm{h}$ to remove part of the solvent, then thermally imidized in a vacuum at $200^{\circ}\mathrm{C} / 1\mathrm{h}$ , $300^{\circ}\mathrm{C} / 1\mathrm{h}$ , and $400^{\circ}\mathrm{C} / 0.5\mathrm{h}$ , respectively, to give a self-supporting CPI film with thickness of around $50~\mu \mathrm{m}$ . A series of CPI- $x$ films with various boron-loadings ( $x$ represents the molar ratio of BACB in diamine monomers and is equal to 0, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50, respectively) were obtained. ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) Figure 1.Cont. ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) Figure 1. (a) Synthesis of the carborane-containing aromatic polyimide (CPI) films; (b) $^{1}\mathrm{H}$ -NMR spectrum of a representative poly(amic acid) (PAA) resin (CPI-50) and (c) FT-IR spectra of the CPI films. ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) The PAA solution of CPI was trickled into excess ethanol to form a reddish-brown precipitate, which was immersed in ethanol for $48\mathrm{h}$ and dried under vacuum at $180^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for $12\mathrm{h}$ to eliminate the NMP. Afterward, the dried PAA resin of CPI was characterized by ${}^{1}\mathrm{H}$ -NMR, XPS, and elemental analysis. Figure 1b shows ${}^{1}\mathrm{H}$ -NMR of a representative PAA resin (PAA-50), which was used for the preparation of CPI-50 film. The distinct splitting patterns were in well accordance with the expected PAA-50 chemical structure. Additionally, Table 1 illustrates the XPS and elemental analysis results of the PAA resin (PAA-50); although the XPS and elemental analysis could not detect specific elements, the contents of other elements were in great agreement with the calculated values. Furthermore, the chemical structures were also characterized by FT-IR spectroscopic measurements (Figure 1c). The intensity of the B-H characteristic absorptions around $2600~\mathrm{cm^{-1}}$ became stronger and stronger with the increasing of boron loadings. The strong absorptions around 1775 and $1720~\mathrm{cm^{-1}}$ referred to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of $\mathrm{C = O}$ bands of imide rings in the polymer backbone. Other absorption bands collected near $1366~\mathrm{cm^{-1}}$ and $3070~\mathrm{cm^{-1}}$ were assigned to C-N and $\mathrm{C_{atom - H}}$ , respectively. No signals around $3300\sim 3500~\mathrm{cm^{-1}}$ were detected in the FT-IR spectra, which meant neither $-\mathrm{NH}_2$ of BACB nor $-\mathrm{CONH}-$ of PAA existed in CPI films. That is, all the BACB was reacted with dianhydride, and the CPI film was completely imidized. Table 1. Calculated and experimental values of each element in the PAA resin (PAA-50).
Elemental ContentsC (%)N (%)H (%)O (%)B (%)
Calculated values61.515.334.1418.7310.29
Elemental analysis62.015.444.02--
XPS analysis60.304.94-19.5511.07
- The elemental analysis cannot detect the contents of B and O; XPS analysis can not detect the content of H, but the contents of other elements were in great agreement with the calculated values. Furthermore, no phase separation was detected by the polarizing microscopic images in all of the CPI films (Figure 2). The CPI films showed homogeneous and transparent images under polarized light, demonstrating that the carborane cage structures were linked into the polymer backbone instead of simply being dispersed as an inorganic filler. Since BACB is a characteristic reddish-brown powder, the color of the PAA solution deepened with increasing carborane loadings, as shown in Figure S1. Therefore, the color of the resultant CPI film deepened with increasing carborane loadings. ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) Figure 2. Polarizing microscopic images of the CPI films (the inserted images were the films covered on the logo of the Chinese Academy of Sciences). 20μm # 3.2. Thermal and Thermo-Oxidative Stabilities Thermal stability characterized by DMA and TGA were compared in Table S1. The $T_{g}$ values (tan $\delta$ in DMA) increased from $304^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (CPI-0) to $371^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (CPI-50) with increasing boron loadings (Figure 3a). Although the bulky carborane cage structure enlarged the distance of polymer chain to chain, it also contrarily restricted the mobility of polymer chains in large-scale due to its big steric hindrance, and the movements induced by larger inter-chain space were too short in size scale to change polymers from a glassy state to rubbery state resulting in increased $T_{g}$ values. ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) Figure 3. Thermal and thermo-oxidative stabilities of the CPI films; (a) DMA curves; (b) TGA curves in air; (c) TGA curves in $\mathrm{N}_2$ . ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) Compared with the boron-free film (CPI-0), incorporating carborane into the polyimide backbone structure greatly enhanced polymer thermal and thermo-oxidative stabilities (Figure 3b,c). Both thermal and thermo-oxidative stability were improved with increasing boron-loadings. The initial thermal decomposition temperature (<1.0 wt.% of the original weight) were detected over $550^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ in air, $50^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ higher than that of CPI-0. The $T_{5}$ in $\mathbf{N}_2$ were measured in the range of $595 - 601^{\circ}\mathrm{C}, 13 - 19^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ higher than CPI-0, and apparently did not change with the boron-loadings. However, the $T_{5}$ in air increased from $602^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (CPI-5) to $632^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (CPI-50) with increasing boron-loadings. At $50\mathrm{mol}.\%$ of carborane loading (CPI-50), the $T_{10}$ was measured at $657^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (in $\mathbf{N}_2$ ) and $749^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (in air), respectively, and the char yield at $750^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ was as high as $87.9\%$ (in $\mathbf{N}_2$ ) and $90.0\%$ (in air), indicating obvious improvement in thermo-oxidative stability. Moreover, the TGA analysis manifested that some weight gains were observed for the CPI films beginning at about $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ in air, which was probably attributed to the oxidation of boron atoms in the carborane cage to produce a boron oxide layer on the film surface, which could retard the further erosion of inner polymers [39]. # 3.3. Mechanical Properties During copolymerization with aromatic dianhydride, BACB is less reactive than the other two aromatic diamines (4,4'-ODA and PDA) due to the strong electron-withdrawing ability and the large steric hindrance effect of the carborane cage structure, diminishing the polymerization reactivity of the diamine monomer. Although it was hard for BACB to form high $M_w$ polymer, the BACB still reacted with dianhydride to form a lower $M_w$ polymer. As a consequence, the molecular weight of PAA resins was reduced, and the polymer inter-chain interaction was weakened with increasing boron-loadings, leading to a decreased tensile strength and modulus (Figure 4). Thus, the CPI-50 film showed the lowest mechanical performance with a tensile strength $(T_{S})$ of $108\mathrm{MPa}$ and tensile modulus $(T_{M})$ of 3.7 GPa, respectively, compared with CPI-20 $(T_{S}:161\mathrm{MPa}, T_{M}:4.9\mathrm{GPa})$ . ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) Figure 4. Tensile modulus and tensile strength of the CPI films vs. boron loadings. CPI films with different boron-loadings (CPI-0, CPI-20, and CPI-50) were thermally treated in a muffle furnace under various thermo-oxidative aging conditions (600 °C/30 min, 600 °C/60 min, 700 °C/5 min, 700 °C/15 min, 700 °C/30 min, etc.). The flexibility of the CPI films was estimated by bend testing, and the tensile properties were measured on an Instron 5567 universal testing machine with a drawing rate of 2.0 mm min $^{-1}$ . Table 2 summarized the results of bend testing, after thermo-oxidative aging at 600 °C for 30 to 60 min, the boron-free films (CPI-0) were damaged into ashes or volatiles due to the violent degradation, and could not be tested. However, all the boron-containing films could be bent $180^{\circ}$ under the same condition, showing great retention of the flexibility (Figure 5a). Additionally, after thermo-oxidative aging at 700 °C/30 min, although the CPI-20 film was broken into two pieces during bend testing, the CPI-50 film still exhibited good flexibility. Table 2. Bending results of the CPI films after thermo-oxidative aging at various conditions.
Temperature/Time600 °C/30 min600 °C/60 min700 °C/5 min700 °C/15 min700 °C/30 min
CPI-0-----
CPI-20+++++++++
CPI-50++++++++++
$\mathbf{+ + }$ : bendable; $^+$ : unbendable but can be conducted on tensile test; -: broken. Regarding the tensile tests, both CPI-20 and CPI-50 films showed measurable tensile strength after thermo-oxidative aging at 600 to $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (Figure 5b); the specific data are displayed in Table S2. The unaged CPI-20 and CPI-50 films have a tensile strength of 161 and $108\mathrm{MPa}$ , respectively. After thermo-oxidative aging at $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C} / 30\mathrm{min}$ , the tensile strength reduced to $88\mathrm{MPa}$ for CPI-20 and $79\mathrm{MPa}$ for CPI-50. When the aging time extended to $60\mathrm{min}$ at $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the tensile strength was reduced to $23\mathrm{MPa}$ (CPI-20) and $42\mathrm{MPa}$ (CPI-50), indicating that the thermo-oxidation at $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ increased with extending aging time. Moreover, after aging at $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for 5, 15, and $30\mathrm{min}$ , the CPI-50 film still kept a tensile strength of 63, 35, and $18\mathrm{MPa}$ , successfully, and its strength retention was much higher than that of CPI-20 (Figure 5c). Compared with boron-free films, the incorporation of a carborane cage into the polyimide backbone structure significantly improved the polymer thermo-oxidative stability at $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) Figure 5. (a) The effects of bend testing; (b) tensile strength, and (c) retention of tensile strength of the CPI film samples after thermo-oxidative aging at various conditions. ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) # 3.4. Mechanism of Thermo-Oxidative Resistance The extraordinary thermo-oxidative stabilities of the CPI films might be attributed to the formation of the boron oxide protective layer on the film surface, preventing the direct contact of inner polymer with oxygen molecules and resulting in the reduced decomposition rate of the inner polymer. Hence, the sheltered inner polymer could still provide the film with certain mechanical strength and flexibility even after thermo-oxidative aging at $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . The thermal degradation kinetics in air was analyzed via TGA with the Flynn-Wall-Ozawa method [40,41], based on the following Equation (2), which is a derivate from Equation (1): $$ \log \beta = \log \frac {A E _ {a}}{R g (\alpha)} - 2. 3 1 5 - \frac {0 . 4 5 7 E _ {a}}{R T}, \tag {1} $$ $$ \frac {\mathrm {d} \log \beta}{\mathrm {d} (1 / T)} = \frac {0 . 4 5 7 E _ {a}}{R}, \tag {2} $$ where $\beta$ is the heating rate (K min $^{-1}$ ), $R$ is the gas constant (8.314 J moL $^{-1}$ K $^{-1}$ ), $E_{a}$ is the activation energy (kJ moL $^{-1}$ ), $T$ is the absolute temperature (K), and $g(\alpha)$ is the function of conversion rate $\alpha$ . TGA of the CPI films with various boron-loadings (CPI-0, CPI-20, CPI-50) were performed in air at different heating rates. Figure 6 compares the DTG (Derivative Thermogravimetry) curves of CPI films with different carborane loadings. The two intersection points of the two tangents of the decomposition peak and baseline were set as initial degradation temperature $(T_{i})$ and terminal degradation temperature $(T_{t})$ . The initial weight residue was regarded as the weight residue at the initial degradation temperature and the same as the terminal weight residue. Then, the section between the initial and terminal weight residue was equally divided into 10 pieces, and each weight residue represented a certain conversion rate $\alpha$ . According to the conversion rate $\alpha$ and heating rate $\beta$ , $T_{\alpha}$ (temperature at conversion rate $\alpha$ ) could be directly determined in the corresponding TGA curves. Subsequently, plots of $\lg \beta$ against $1000 / T$ were linearly fitted to a series of straight lines in which the slopes were used to compute the activation energy $E_{a}$ at the corresponding conversion rate $\alpha$ , as exhibited in Figure 7a. All of the generated lines were in excellent linear dependence with $\mathbb{R}^2 >0.98$ , as summarized in Table S3. ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) Figure 6. DTG curves of (a) CPI-0, (b) CPI-20, and (c) CPI-50 under various heating rates. ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) Figure 7. (a) Flynn-Wall-Ozawa plots for CPI-20 and (b) activation energy against conversion rate in the CPI films. ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) Obviously, the incorporation of carborane lowered the initial activation energy of the polyimide system (Figure 7b). Since the degradation activation energy $E_{a}$ was defined as the energy barrier that should be overcome to activate the thermal decomposition, the introduction of a flexible, bulky carborane cage loosened the chain packing, resulting in the lower initial $E_{a}$ . With an increase in oxidation time, the $E_{a}$ of CPI-0 diminished drastically while that of CPI-50 ascended gradually, probably due to the formation of a boron oxide protective layer during the decomposition. The formed boron oxide layer covered on the film surface could shelter the inner polymer, thus enhancing the energy barrier. In addition, the increase in the energy barrier, in turn, slowed down the decomposition process of the inner polymer, and the degradation rate decreased little by little as decomposition proceeded. At the conversion rate $\alpha > 0.5$ , the $E_{a}$ of CPI-50 exceeded that of CPI-0, and it continued to grow while the $E_{a}$ of CPI-0 continued to diminish (Figure 7b). Furthermore, the $E_{a}$ of CPI-20 film declined at first and then rose up later, implying that the boron content was too low to yield a sufficient protective layer to enhance the $E_{a}$ at the beginning. Compared with CPI-0, the protective layer only retarded the degradation rate of CPI-20, but when the conversion rate $\alpha > 0.6$ , the layer was dense enough to protect the inner polymer, leading to the growth of $E_{a}$ in which the value was between CPI-0 and CPI-50. To further investigate the mechanism, three representatives with different carbon-loadings (CPI-0, CPI-20, and CPI-50) were isothermally aged by TGA in air at $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for $150\mathrm{min}$ (Figure 8a). Apparently, the thermo-oxidative stability was greatly improved by increasing the carborane loadings. Since the boron-free CPI film (CPI-0) was completely decomposed into volatiles rapidly, whereas the CPI-20 and CPI-50 film still maintained more than $29\%$ and $76\%$ weight residue after $150\mathrm{min}$ of thermo-oxidative degradation at $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , respectively. In addition, the boron-free CPI film (CPI-0) was easier to decompose with a degradation peak more intense than the carborane-containing films (CPI-20 and CPI-50). Combined with DTG curves (Figure 8b), a second sharp weight loss peak immediately followed the first for CPI-0 in air. However, only one decomposition peak was observed for CPI-50 in air. This could be interpreted by the fact that the further degradation of the CPI-50 films was postponed due to the higher $E_{a}$ of the boron oxide passivation layer generated during the thermal treatment. It was noteworthy that a new broad peak was generated between the sharp decomposition peak and the slow degradation region for CPI-20 in the DTG curves (Figure 8c), manifesting that there was a distinctive degradation from 5 to 60 min in isothermal treatment. Figure 8. (a) TGA and (b) DTG curves of CPI-0, CPI-20, and CPI-50 isothermally treated at $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for $150\mathrm{min}$ in air, and (c) three degradation stages of CPI-20 film. XPS was measured to investigate the chemical changes in the binding energies of boron and oxygen elements of the CPI films thermally treated at different conditions (Figure 9). The test samples of the CPI-20 film were cleaned by deionized water and ethanol for several times to wash out the impurities on the surfaces and then heated to $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ in a muffle furnace standing for various times (5 min, 15 min, 30 min, 60 min, and 120 min). After thermo-oxidative aging for 5 min, the polyimide located on the out of surface was initially decomposed, evidenced by the disappearance of the carborane cage peaks (189.6 eV) and $\mathrm{C = O}$ bonds (531.6 eV), respectively, and the absorption intensities of C-O (533.3 eV) in the O 1s spectrum became more conspicuous. Furthermore, the signal that occurred around $192.2\mathrm{eV}$ was assigned to the partially destroyed carborane cage, owing to the pyrolysis [42]. Meanwhile, the boron atoms linked in the carborane cage were reacted with oxygen to form a boron oxide layer on the surface evidenced by the appearance of B-O bonds in B 1s (193.4 eV) and O 1s (532.2 eV) spectra in the initial 5 min. Then, a dense passivation layer was covered on the surface, because only the B-O peak was observed both in B 1s and O 1s spectra (15 min). Nevertheless, when the aging time extended to $30\mathrm{min}$ , the partially broken carborane cage (192.3 eV) and C-O (533.3 eV) signals were reappeared in B 1s and O 1s spectra, respectively, which was in well accordance with the fact that the degradation rate of CPI-20 during 5 to $60\mathrm{min}$ was faster than that of CPI-50 while slower than CPI-0's. It was presumed that, during 5 to $60\mathrm{min}$ , there was a dynamic equilibrium in the breakage and regeneration of the passivation layer, as a result of the disappearance and reappearance of partially damaged carborane cage and C-O peaks. With the aging time increased, the boron oxide layer became increasingly denser, evidenced by the fact that only the B-O peak was collected. ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) Figure 9. (a) B 1s and (b) O 1s XPS spectra of CPI-20 isothermally treated at $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) Figure 10 compares the surface morphologies of CPI-20 samples after thermo-oxidative aging at $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for different times. The thermal degradation could be divided into three stages, as shown in Figure 11. Based on the XPS analysis, the first stage was the decomposition of the external polymer, and the surface was gradually rougher. In this stage, the outmost film was reacted with oxygen, and the volatiles, such as oxynitride, oxycarbide, and hydroxide, were eliminated, accompanied by the formation of a B-O bond. The second stage was the generation of the boron oxide protective layer on the film surface. Initially, part of the surface was covered by that oxidative layer, as shown in Figure 9 (5 min) and Figure 10b. Then, the polymer next to the opened hole on the outmost passivation layer was degraded and formed a new passivation layer in situ, which enabled the hybrid polyimide surface to be "self-healing" [43], as shown in Figure 9. In this stage, breakage and regeneration of the passivation layer were in a dynamic equilibrium, but the regenerated layers gradually repaired the breakage of the previous one, ultimately forming the multilayered protective layer (Figure 10e). This layer covered the film surface and prevented the direct contact of the inner polymer and oxygen, thus significantly reducing the degradation rate. Although a "self-healing", multilayered, dense passivation layer was generated on the film surface during the second stage, the heat could still be transferred into the interior via the protective layer, so the polymer was slowly degraded in the last stage. ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) Figure 10. SEM images of the surfaces of CPI-20 samples heated at $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ in air for (a) $0\mathrm{min}$ ; (b) $5\mathrm{min}$ ; (c) $15\mathrm{min}$ ; (d) $30\mathrm{min}$ ; (e) $60\mathrm{min}$ , and (f) $120\mathrm{min}$ . ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) Figure 11. Mechanism of the three stages during thermal degradation.

file_name: Carborane-Containing Aromatic Polyimide Films with Ultrahigh Thermo-Oxidative Stability.md
content:
# 4. Conclusions

Carborane-containing polyimide (CPI) films with ultrahigh thermal and thermo-oxidative stability up to  $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  were successfully synthesized. The excellent thermal and thermo-oxidative stability was attributed to the multilayered protection layers formed on the film surface by thermal conversion of the carborane group into boron oxides. The boron oxide layer enhanced the degradation activation energy and suppressed the direct contact of inner polymer materials with oxygen molecules in a high-temperature environment, acting as a "self-healing" skin layer on the polyimide materials. Compared with the corresponding carborane-free films, the CPI films retained much higher mechanical strength and flexibility even after thermo-oxidative aging at  $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C} / 30\mathrm{min}$ . It was this "self-healing", multilayered, dense boron oxide protective layer that slowed down the further erosion process of the inner polymer and prolonged the service life of materials at high temperatures.

Supplementary Materials: The following are available online at http://www.mdpi.com/2073-4360/11/12/1930/s1, Figure S1: The images of the carborane containing PAA solutions, Table S1: Thermal properties of the CPI films, Table S2: Tensile properties of CPI-20 and CPI-50 after thermo-oxidative aging, Table S3: Degradation  $E_{a}$  and linearly dependent coefficients  $\mathbb{R}^2$  of CPI-0, CPI-20 and CPI-50 and Scheme S1: Synthesis of 1,7-bis (aminophenyl)-meta-carborane (BACB).

Author Contributions: Investigation and writing—original draft preparation, F.L.; writing—review and editing, S.Y. and H.Y.; supervision, S.Y. and H.Y.; funding acquisition, S.Y., H.Y. and G.F.; validation, X.Z., Z.Z. and G.F.; project administration, S.Y.

Funding: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC grant 51973225 and 51573094).

Conflicts of Interest: The authors declare no conflict of interest.

file_name: Influence of Surface Modified MWCNTs on the Mechanical, Electrical and Thermal Properties of Polyimide Nanocom.md
section_title: Influence of Surface Modified MWCNTs on the Mechanical, Electrical and Thermal Properties of Polyimide Nanocomposites
content:
[MINERU_DISCARDED_META]
Nanoscale Res Lett (2008) 3:444-453 DOI 10.1007/s11671-008-9179-4
NANO EXPRESS
[/MINERU_DISCARDED_META]

# Influence of Surface Modified MWCNTs on the Mechanical, Electrical and Thermal Properties of Polyimide Nanocomposites

B. P. Singh  $\cdot$  Deepankar Singh  $\cdot$  R. B. Mathur  $\cdot$  T. L. Dhami

Received: 30 June 2008 / Accepted: 16 September 2008 / Published online: 15 October 2008  
© to the authors 2008

Abstract Polyamic acid, the precursor of polyimide, was used for the preparation of polyimide/multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) nanocomposite films by solvent casting technique. In order to enhance the chemical compatibility between polyimide matrix and MWCNTs, the latter was surface modified by incorporating acidic and amide groups by chemical treatment with nitric acid and octadecylamine  $(\mathrm{C_{18}H_{39}N})$ , respectively. While the amide-MWCNT/polyimide composite shows higher mechanical properties at low loadings  $(< 3\mathrm{wt}\%)$ , the acid-MWCNT/ polyimide composites perform better at higher loadings  $(5\mathrm{wt}\%)$ . The tensile strength (TS) and the Young's modulus (YM) values of the acid-MWCNT/polyimide composites at  $5\mathrm{wt}\%$  MWCNT loadings was 151 and  $3360\mathrm{MPa}$ , respectively, an improvement of  $54\%$  in TS and  $35\%$  in YM over the neat polyimide film  $(\mathrm{TS} = 98\mathrm{MPa};\mathrm{YM} = 2492\mathrm{MPa})$ . These MWCNT-reinforced composites show remarkable improvement in terms of thermal stability as compared to that for pure polyimide film. The electrical conductivity of  $5\mathrm{wt}\%$  acid modified MWCNTs/polyimide nanocomposites improved to  $0.94~\mathrm{S~cm^{-1}}$ $(6.67\times 10^{-18}\mathrm{S~cm^{-1}}$  for pure polyimide) the maximum achieved so far for MWCNT-polyimide composites.

Keywords Multiwalled carbon nanotubes  $\cdot$  Polyimide  $\cdot$  Nanocomposites  $\cdot$  Electrical properties  $\cdot$  Mechanical properties

file_name: Influence of Surface Modified MWCNTs on the Mechanical, Electrical and Thermal Properties of Polyimide Nanocom.md
content:
# Introduction

After their discovery in 1991 by Iijima [1], carbon nanotubes (CNTs) have attracted considerable interest because of their unique as well as superior physical, electrical, magnetic, chemical stability, thermal conductivity and mechanical properties [2]. Due to their exceptionally high aspect ratio and mechanical properties, incorporation of small amounts of CNTs into a polymer matrix is expected to enhance the properties of the resulting nanocomposites more than any existing material. The most critical issue of CNTs/polymer nanocomposites is the adhesion/compatibility between the nanotubes and polymer which ultimately controls the interface between the CNTs and the polymer matrix. Unfortunately pure CNTs are insoluble in any organic solvents and they tend to form agglomerates because of strong Van der Wall forces which results in negative effects on the properties of the resulting nanocomposites. As such achieving a high degree of dispersion of CNTs in any polymer matrix is quite a challenging task. Chemical functionalization is the simplest and widely accepted method to improve the compatibility between CNTs and polymer in which CNTs are treated with strong acids like nitric acid and sulphuric acid or combination of both. The functionalized CNTs contain carboxylic and hydroxyl functional groups and are soluble in most of the organic solvents [3].

Polyimide is a high performance specialty class of polymers of aromatic nature with chemical structure  $-\mathrm{R}(\mathrm{CO})\mathrm{N}-$ , which comes under the family of ladder polymers, due to their flexibility, high strength, superior thermal stability and dielectric properties. As a result it is used in applications ranging from adhesives, thermal resistant coatings, high performance composites, fibres, foams, membranes, mouldings and films [4]. It can be used

up to  $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  for short duration and prolonged use between 200 and  $350^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  without much deterioration in mechanical and other properties. Polyimides are produced by the condensation reaction of an aromatic dianhydride and aromatic diamine to form an oligomer of amic acid known as polyamic acid which act as a precursor for polyimide and can be further cyclized (imidization) by thermal means to produce polyimide. Out of the many types of aromatic polyimides produced worldwide, the largest market and most preferred polyimide for various applications is based on the pyromellatic dianhydride (PMDA) and  $4,4^{\prime}$ -oxydianiline (ODA) [4].

In recent times considerable effort has been devoted in the field of preparation and study of CNTs/polyimide nanocomposites from various angles ranging from highly conductive to super strong CNTs/polyimide nanocomposites. Addressing the problem of poor dispersion of CNTs in the polyimide matrix, Park et al. [5] used in situ polymerization technique to disperse unmodified single wall carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) in polyimide. Ouanies et al. [6] studied the electrical properties of SWCNTs doped polyimide nanocomposites. Zhu et al. [7] described a method for achieving enhanced dispersion of multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) in the polyimide matrix by the acid treatment of MWCNTs. However, they reported a slight decrease in the thermal properties of the nanocomposites due to acid modification. Mo et al. [3] employed in situ technique to disperse functionalized MWCNTs in polyimide matrix and reported a maximum achievable strength of  $165.5\mathrm{MPa}$  at  $7\mathrm{wt}\%$  loading of functionalized MWCNTs. Yuen et al. concluded that by using amino functionalized MWCNTs the electrical properties of the MWCNTs/polyimide nanocomposites are enhanced considerably; unfortunately, it resulted in decreased tensile strength (TS) due to a possibility of reaction between the amino functionalized MWCNTs with polyamic acid [8]. Hu et al. also studied amino functionalized MWCNTs/ polyimide nanocomposites using in situ polymerization method to disperse MWCNTs in the polyimide matrix [9].

Looking at the above scenario, it seems that it is difficult to achieve significant improvement in both the electrical as well as mechanical properties of the CNT/polyimide

composites simultaneously. A new type of amino functional groups was therefore grafted on the MWCNTs by treating them with octadecylamine  $(\mathrm{C}_{18}\mathrm{H}_{39}\mathrm{N})$ . The grafted functional group consists of a secondary amide attached to the MWCNTs along with a long alkyl chain. The long alkyl chain helped in preventing agglomeration and bundling of MWCNTs due to repulsive force between the alkyl chain, producing a stable and homogeneous dispersion of MWCNTs. We report here that these amino functionalized MWCNTs reinforced polyimide nanocomposites not only exhibit improved mechanical properties, but also the electrical properties much superior to the one reported so far.

file_name: Influence of Surface Modified MWCNTs on the Mechanical, Electrical and Thermal Properties of Polyimide Nanocom.md
content:
# Experimental

# Materials

MWCNTs were synthesized using toluene as a carbon source and ferrocene as catalyst precursor on a CVD set-up established in the laboratory [10]. The MWCNTs produced inside the reactor  $40 - 70\mathrm{nm}$  in diameter and  $50 - 100~{\mu\mathrm{m}}$  long. These were  $90\%$  pure with  $<  10$  wt.% of catalyst Fe. Polyamic acid (ABRON-S 10) based on pyromellitic dianhydride (PMDA) and 4,4-oxydianiline (ODA) was procured from M/s ABR organics Ltd., India (Fig. 1). It was obtained as a  $10\%$  solution in DMAc  $(N,N$  dimethylacetamide), with a solid content of  $10 - 12\%$  .It is a precursor form of polyimide (PI) polymer and was kept at  $0^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  during this work. Octadecylamine  $\left(\mathrm{C}_{18}\mathrm{H}_{39}\mathrm{N}\right)$  AR grade was supplied by M/s Across Organics Co., USA with a purity of  $90\%$

file_name: Influence of Surface Modified MWCNTs on the Mechanical, Electrical and Thermal Properties of Polyimide Nanocom.md
content:
# Acid Modification of MWCNTs

Two grams of MWCNTs were treated with  $65\%$  (v/v)  $\mathrm{HNO}_3$  for  $48\mathrm{h}$  in a refluxing apparatus [11] with constant stirring to convert the MWCNTs to acid functionalized (MWCNTs-COOH). The treated material was washed several times with deionized water till washings were neutral to pH paper and dried at  $100^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  for  $12\mathrm{h}$  prior to use.

![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片)  
Fig. 1 Structure of PMDA/ ODA-based polyamic acid

Amine Modification of Acid Modified

A  $100~\mathrm{mL}$  -flask was charged with  $1\mathrm{g}$  of MWCNTs-COOH dispersed in  $30~\mathrm{mL}$  of anhydrous benzene  $(\mathrm{C_6H_6})$  to which  $30~\mathrm{mL}$  of thionyl chloride  $(\mathrm{SOCl}_2)$  was subsequently added and stirred at  $70^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  for  $8\mathrm{h}$ . After the reaction, the whole reaction mixture was filtered and the solid residue (MWCNTs-COCl) was washed several times with anhydrous THF and dried at  $50^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  overnight. Approximately  $0.5\mathrm{g}$  of MWCNTs-COCl was suspended in THF and was stirred in excess of octadecylamine  $(\mathrm{C_{18}H_{39}N})$  for about  $90\mathrm{h}$  to produce amine modified MWCNTs (MWCNTsCO-NH-  $\mathrm{C_{18}H_{37}}$  ), the whole reaction mixture was maintained at a temperature of  $100^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . After functionalization reaction, MWCNTs were washed with ethanol to dissolve excess octadecylamine and subsequently with deionized water followed by drying under vacuum prior to use [12].

Nanocomposite Fabrication

Appropriate amount of acid or amine functionalized MWCNTs were separately mixed in  $10~\mathrm{mL}$  of DMAc by means of ultrasonication at room temperature to obtain a homogenous suspension of MWCNTs/DMAc. This suspension was poured into the polyamic acid and vigorously stirred with the help of a magnetic stirrer (80 rpm at  $150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) for  $40\mathrm{min}$  to obtain a highly viscous suspension of MWCNTs in polyamic acid. The viscous mass was casted on to a clean glass plate and dried in an air circulating oven at a temperature of  $70^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  for  $12\mathrm{h}$  to evaporate the solvent (DMAc) and to obtain a dried tack free film. Imidization (curing) of nanocomposites film was achieved by heating the nanocomposites film in an air circulating oven at 100, 150, 200, and  $250^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  for  $1\mathrm{h}$  at each respective temperature with a final heat treatment at  $300^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  for  $2\mathrm{h}$ . During imidization, the amide linkage converted in to imide linkage (cyclization) with the evolution of water molecules. The whole scheme of nanocomposites fabrication is illustrated in Fig. 2.

file_name: Influence of Surface Modified MWCNTs on the Mechanical, Electrical and Thermal Properties of Polyimide Nanocom.md
content:
# Characterization

Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy

Functionalization of MWCNTs and conversion of polymeric acid to polyimide (imidization) was confirmed by the Fourier transform infrared spectra recorded on a Perkin Elmer spectrum BX FTIR spectrometer.

Scanning Electron Microscopy

The surface morphology of the as-produced, functionalized MWCNTs and the fractured surface of the nanocomposites

was analysed on a Scanning Electron Microscope (Leo model: S-440).

file_name: Influence of Surface Modified MWCNTs on the Mechanical, Electrical and Thermal Properties of Polyimide Nanocom.md
content:
# Thermoxidative Stability

Thermal stability of the nanocomposites was examined by using a Thermo gravimetric analyser (Mettler Toledo TGA/SDTA  $851^{\mathrm{e}^{*}}$ ). The test was performed between 50 and  $900^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  at a heating rate of  $10^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}$  in air with a flow rate of  $50~\mathrm{cc / min}$ .

file_name: Influence of Surface Modified MWCNTs on the Mechanical, Electrical and Thermal Properties of Polyimide Nanocom.md
content:
# Mechanical Properties

The tensile strength (TS) and Young's modulus (YM) of the nanocomposites were measured on an Instron machine, Model 4411, at room temperature using ASTM-D882 test method. The test samples were cut into the strips of size  $100\mathrm{mm} \times 25\mathrm{mm} \times 0.1\mathrm{mm}$ . The gauge length was kept as  $50\mathrm{mm}$ , while the cross-head speed was maintained at  $2\mathrm{mm/min}$ . The mechanical properties were evaluated using the built-in software in the machine. A minimum of five tests were performed for each composite sample and their average is reported.

file_name: Influence of Surface Modified MWCNTs on the Mechanical, Electrical and Thermal Properties of Polyimide Nanocom.md
content:
# Electrical Conductivity

The electrical conductivity of the composite films  $(100\mathrm{mm}\times 25\mathrm{mm}\times 0.1\mathrm{mm})$  was measured by fourpoint contact method [13] using a Keithley 224 programmable current source for providing current, the voltage drop was measured by Keithley 197A auto ranging digital microvoltmeter. The values reported in text are averaged over five readings of voltage drops at different portions of the sample.

file_name: Influence of Surface Modified MWCNTs on the Mechanical, Electrical and Thermal Properties of Polyimide Nanocom.md
content:
# Result and Discussion

# FTIR Studies

Figure 3 shows the FTIR spectra of acid and amino functionalized MWCNTs. In the acid functionalized MWCNTs, the peaks at 1634 and  $1295~\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$  correspond to  $\mathrm{C = O}$  and C-O stretching, respectively. The two weak peaks at 2917 and  $2847~\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$  correspond to the -CH stretching mode. A broad peak corresponding to strong absorption at 3433 and  $3150~\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$  can be ascribed to -OH stretching vibration in -COOH group. In the amino functionalized MWCNTs, a strong and broad peak at  $1631~\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$  with a shoulder at  $1707~\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$  are ascribed to  $\mathrm{C = O}$  stretching vibration due to the formation of amide linkage. The two strong peaks at 2918 and  $2847~\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$  with a third band appearing at

![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片)  
Fig. 2 Preparation scheme of polyimide/MWCNTs nanocomposites by the solvent casting method

$2954~\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$  are ascribed to  $-\mathrm{CH}$  stretching of the long alkyl chain of octadecylamine. Another peak at  $1184~\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$  corresponds to C-N stretching of amide group. Broad and strong peak with a shoulder at 3440,  $3124~\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$  should correspond to  $-\mathrm{NH}$  stretching of the amine group.

For comparison, the FTIR spectra of the neat polyimide film is shown in Fig. 4a. A strong and broad peak at  $1395~\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$  and another at  $3556~\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$  are ascribed to the C-N stretching of imide ring. Characteristic strong peak due to  $\mathrm{C = O}$  stretching appears at  $1720~\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$ , while asymmetric stretching of  $\mathrm{C = O}$  as a weak peak at  $1777~\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$ . The peak at  $725~\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$  corresponds to bending vibration of  $\mathrm{C = O}$  group. C-C stretching of the aromatic ring appears at  $1515~\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$ . The studies confirm the formation of polyimide and match well with the earlier reported data [14].

Figure 4b shows the FTIR spectrum of amide-MWCNTs reinforced polyimide composites. The spectrum matches closely with that of neat polyimide as shown in Fig. 4a. This is further confirmed by the absence of broad peak around the  $3200~\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$  due to -COOH functional

groups which gets converted into polyimide. The IR spectra of both neat polyimide as well as MWCNT/poly-imide composites also compare well with earlier reported data [14].

file_name: Influence of Surface Modified MWCNTs on the Mechanical, Electrical and Thermal Properties of Polyimide Nanocom.md
content:
# Surface Morphology of the MWCNTs

Figure 5(a-c) shows the SEM micrographs of the MWCNTs of the as-produced and the functionalized tubes. As seen from Fig. 5a, the raw MWCNTs are produced in the form of agglomerated bundles. These bundles are broken down into separate tubes due to violent refluxing during acid and amine functionalization (Fig. 5b, c). Treatment of MWCNTs with strong acids usually shortens the length of the MWCNTs, but in the present case we do not observe any such shortening of the tubes. A closer look at the micrographs reveal that the amino functionalized MWCNTs are relatively well dispersed as compared to raw and acid functionalized MWCNTs (Fig. 5a, c). This could be due to the repulsive forces generated between the long alkyl chain of the amino functional group.

![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片)  
Fig. 3 FTIR spectra of as such MWCNTs, acid modified MWCNTs and amine modified MWCNTs

A typical microstructure of the fractured surface of the  $2\mathrm{wt}\%$  MWCNT acid-MWCNT/polyimide and amineMWCNT/polyimide nanocomposite is shown in Figs. 6a and b, respectively. Though the MWCNTs show a high degree of dispersion throughout the polyimide matrix in both the cases; the amino functionalized MWCNTs are completely coated with the polymer (polyimide) and remain well embedded in the polyimide matrix, the acid modified tubes show very small amount of polymer coating. This feature clearly indicates better interfacial adhesion between the amino functionalized MWCNTs and matrix as compared to acid functionalized MWCNTs.

file_name: Influence of Surface Modified MWCNTs on the Mechanical, Electrical and Thermal Properties of Polyimide Nanocom.md
content:
# Thermoxidative Properties

Figure 7a clearly shows the onset of the degradation temperature of the raw, acid and amine modified MWCNTs. In the case of as produced MWCNTs, the weight loss starts at  $475^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , whereas in case of modified tubes the weight loss initiation temperatures shifts to around  $300^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  for acid modified and  $230^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  for amine modified tubes. The curves of the modified tubes show the weight loss in two steps, the initial one before  $400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  is due to decomposition of the functional groups, while the other one relates to the oxidation of the nanotubes. A weight loss of  $\sim 17\%$  for acid-MWCNTs and  $\sim 40\%$  for amide-MWCNTs is observed which quantitatively estimates these functional groups to 6 and  $5\mathrm{mmol / g}$ , respectively. These values are quite high as compared to previous results from Reto et al. [12], Hu et al. [9] and Datsyuk et al. [15].

The weight loss behaviour of the respective composites with different weight fractions of functionalized MWCNTs are presented in Fig.7b and c. It is clear from the TGA curves that with increase in the acid-MWCNTs loading,

degradation temperature  $(T_{\mathrm{d}})$  also increases up to  $3\mathrm{wt}\%$  which is due to the synergistic of polyimide and MWCNTs wherein large volume of polyamic acid is converted into polyimide. However, when MWCNTs loading is increased to  $5\mathrm{wt}\%$  slight decrease in the  $T_{\mathrm{d}}$  is observed. This can be attributed to the interference produced by the large number of acidic groups present on the MWCNTs which reduces the percentage conversion of polyamic acid to polyimide. In case of amide-MWCNTs/polyimide, nanocomposites increase in MWCNTs loading does not seem to show much increment in the  $T_{\mathrm{d}}$ , which is explained by the fact that the amine group present onto the amine-MWCNTs inhibits the imidization process [7, 8]. Table 1 shows the numerical values of the  $T_{\mathrm{d}}$  at different loadings. The table also provides degradation temperature corresponding to  $5\%$  wt loss  $(T_{5})$  values of the composites, showing similar trend as  $T_{\mathrm{d}}$ .

file_name: Influence of Surface Modified MWCNTs on the Mechanical, Electrical and Thermal Properties of Polyimide Nanocom.md
content:
# Mechanical Properties

The TS and YM of the nanocomposites with different MWCNT loadings are plotted in Figs. 8a and b, respectively. It is very much evident from the plots that there is a sharp rise in the mechanical properties at small loadings of functionalized MWCNTs. The increase is more gradual with higher loadings ( $>3\mathrm{wt}\%$ ). The difference in the TS values of the two composites is maximum at  $1\mathrm{wt}\%$  loading, wherein the amide-MWCNT/polyimide composite strength is  $136\mathrm{MPa}$  as compared to only  $110\mathrm{MPa}$  for acid-MWNT/polyimide composite. The difference in the TS values narrows down with higher loadings of the tubes so much so that at  $5\mathrm{wt}\%$  of MWCNTs, the TS of acid-MWCNT/polyimide composite becomes higher ( $151\mathrm{MPa}$ ) relative to amide-MWCNTs/polyimide composites ( $126\mathrm{MPa}$ ). This is very close to the value  $165.5\mathrm{MPa}$

![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片)  
Fig. 4 FTIR spectra of a neat polyimide and b 2 wt% amide-MWCNTs reinforced polyimide composite

![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片)

reported only by Mo et al. [3] with  $7\mathrm{wt}\%$  CNT loadings in polyimide. The fracture surfaces of the two composites are completely different. While in case of amine functionalized tubes a thick coating of the polymer matrix (Fig. 6b) is observed, in case of acid modified tubes such coating is either much thinner or even absent (Fig. 6a). Most of amine functionalized tubes remain embedded in the matrix, with few exceptions of MWCNT pull outs. Interestingly the pull out tubes show a telescopic failure (shown by arrows) and suggestive of sharing of load by the tubes before fracture due to strong MWCNT-polyimide bonding. There can be two reasons for lowering the strength at

higher loadings. Firstly, it can be traced to longer chains of amine groups present on the surface of the tubes. The longer alkyl chains provide more repulsion between the individual MWCNT, hence the available surface area for of the MWCNTs will be more as compared to acid-MWCNTs. The corresponding amount of polymer will not be sufficient to properly wet the large volume of MWCNTs surface, therefore creating voids in the composites. Secondly, the amine group present on the surface of MWCNTs may react with the polyamic acid and lower the imidization of polyamic acid to polyimide. The unreacted polyamic acid may be a cause of premature failure of the composites.

![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片)

![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片)  
Fig. 6 SEM fractographs of a  $2\mathrm{wt}\%$  acid modified-MWCNTs/ polyimide nanocomposites and b  $2\mathrm{wt}\%$  amide-MWCNTs/polyimide nanocomposites

![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片)  
Fig. 5 SEM images of a raw, b acid modified-MWCNTs and c amide-MWCNTs

As in the case of TS, the YM of the amide-MWCNTs/polyimide is also higher (3.5 GPa) up to 3 wt% of MWCNT loadings as compared to only 2.75 GPa for acid-MWCNTs/polyimide nanocomposites. However, the value is much higher as compared to neat polyimide film (YM = 2.4 GPa). The study shows that the mechanical properties obtained with amide-MWCNTs/polyimide and that of acid-MWCNTs/polyimide composites are almost

![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片)

![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片)

equivalent, the only difference being that these are achieved with only  $3\mathrm{wt}\%$  of MWCNT in case of former and with  $5\mathrm{wt}\%$  in case of the latter.

file_name: Influence of Surface Modified MWCNTs on the Mechanical, Electrical and Thermal Properties of Polyimide Nanocom.md
content:
# Electrical Properties One unique property of the MWCNTs is their electrical conductivity because of their large aspect ratio and mobile $\pi$ electrons. Addition of very small quantities of MWCNTs significantly increases the conductivity of the composite as observed in Fig. 9. As in the case of mechanical properties, there is a noticeable difference in the electrical conductivity of the two composites at $1\mathrm{wt}\%$ loading. The conductivity is almost twice for amide-MWCNTs/polyamide as compared to acid-MWCNTs/polyamide. The maximum value of the conductivities with $5\mathrm{wt}\%$ acid-MWCNTs $(0.938\mathrm{Scm}^{-1})$ and $5\mathrm{wt}\%$ amide-MWCNTs $(1.032\mathrm{Scm}^{-1})$ are much higher than the previously reported values in the literature by Yuen et al. $(3.76\times 10^{-8}$ for acid modified at $6.98\mathrm{wt}\%$ ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) Fig. 7 TGA curves for a MWCNTs, b acid-MWCNTs/ polyimide nanocomposites and c amide-MWCNTs/polyimide nanocomposites ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) Table 1 Thermal degradation temperature $\left( {T}_{\mathrm{d}}\right)$ and ${T}_{5}$ of MWCNTs/polyimide nanocomposites
Temperature (℃)Pure PIAcid-MWCNTs/polyimide (MWCNTs wt%)Amide-MWCNTs/polyimide (MWCNTs wt%)
0.512350.51235
*Td510515530540570555525520520525525
**T5540545555565585580553550550550550
* Temperature at which degradation starts ** Temperature at which 5 wt% of the material has degraded ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) Fig. 9 Effect of MWCNTs contents on the electrical conductivity of the MWCNTs/polyimide nanocomposites ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片) Fig. 8 a Tensile strength and b Young's modulus of MWCNTs/ polyimide nanocomposites at different MWCNTs loadings $5.78 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ for amine modified at $6.98 \mathrm{wt} \%$ [8]). The neat polymer film, however, behaves as an insulator as also measured by previous authors ( $6.53 \times 10^{-18} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ [8]). A possible explanation for these observations is the high aspect ratio of the MWCNTs used in this study which ranges between 1000 and 3000. The authors in previous studies have used tubes with aspect ratio of almost 400 (diameter $40 - 60 \mathrm{~nm}$ , length $0.5 - 40 \mu \mathrm{m}$ ) which could be the reason for lower electrical conductivity of these composites. Moreover, in the present study the tubes remain intact and are not damaged during surface modification as observed in the SEM micrographs (Fig 5b, c). ![待补充图片](https://placeholder.pics/svg/1x1/FFFFFF/FFFFFF?text=暂无图片)

file_name: Influence of Surface Modified MWCNTs on the Mechanical, Electrical and Thermal Properties of Polyimide Nanocom.md
content:
# Conclusion

Multiwalled carbon nanotubes were successfully functionalized with acid and amino groups. The functionalization resulted in uniform dispersion of the tube during imidization process which resulted in strong bonding and thick coating of the matrix. The fracture behaviour of amine functionalized MWCNT nanocomposites show a strong bonding of the tubes with the matrix which resulted in  $\sim 50\%$  improvement in the TS and  $35\%$  improvement in the YM over the neat polyimide films. The electrical conductivity of the MWCNT reinforced polyimide composites was  $0.938~\mathrm{S~cm}^{-1}$  (5 wt% acid-MWCNTs) and  $1.032~\mathrm{S~cm}^{-1}$  (5 wt% amide-MWCNTs) is the highest achieved so far for polyimide composites. High electrical conductivity together with high strength and improved thermal stability makes these nanocomposites a promising candidate for high-temperature EMI shielding materials.

Acknowledgements The authors are grateful to Prof. Vikram Kumar, Director, NPL, and Dr. A.K. Gupta, Head Engineering Materials Division, for permission to publish the research work. The authors would like to thank Mr. R.K. Seth for carrying out the TGA studies, Mr. Jay Tawale for SEM observation and Ms. Chetna Dhand for carrying out the FTIR studies.

section_title: Polyimide-Derived Supramolecular Systems Containing Various Amounts of Azochromophore for Optical Storage Uses
content:
